title: Ergodic properties of recurrent solutions of stochastic evolution equations
overall_summary: Maslowski and Seidler prove recurrence-based ergodic and uniqueness results for semilinear stochastic evolution equations dX = (AX + f(X))dt + dW in a Hilbert space H, under hypotheses ensuring that solutions actually evolve continuously in a smaller Banach space E compactly embedded into H, and that the transition semigroup is strong Feller and irreducible. The central method is Khasminskii's embedded-cycle construction: choose a bounded recurrent set, build a Markov chain from successive returns to its boundary after excursions, prove the chain is strong Feller and irreducible, and use expected occupation measures over one cycle to construct invariant measures for the continuous-time process. The main dichotomy is that, under assumptions A1-A6, a recurrent process has a unique σ-finite invariant measure up to scalar, while a nonrecurrent process has no finite invariant measure; hence in this framework there is at most one invariant probability measure. The paper is useful for the original problem because it gives a route to uniqueness of invariant measures that can avoid directly identifying the invariant measure: verify an appropriate strong Feller property, support/irreducibility, compact smoothing, and recurrence or use the recurrent/nonrecurrent dichotomy. Its Example 3.10 is especially relevant as it checks the abstract assumptions for one-dimensional stochastic reaction-diffusion equations with Dirichlet boundary conditions and space-time white noise, though only for globally Lipschitz drift.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Standing framework and assumptions A1-A6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper studies the semilinear stochastic evolution equation dX(t) = (AX(t) + f(X(t)))dt + dW_t, X(0)=x, in a real separable Hilbert space H. Here A generates a strongly continuous semigroup S(t) on H; W is a cylindrical Wiener process with bounded self-adjoint strictly positive covariance operator Q; f maps a Banach space E into H; and E is a separable Banach space compactly embedded into H. Assumption A1 is ∫_0^T |S(t)Q^{1/2}|_{HS}^2 dt < ∞ for some T>0. Assumption A2 is that S restricted to E is a strongly continuous semigroup on E and the stochastic convolution Z(t)=∫_0^t S(t-r)dW_r has an E-valued E-continuous modification with the usual Markov independent-increment property Z(t+r)-S(r)Z(t). Assumption A3 says f:E→H is H-Lipschitz on bounded subsets of E and the deterministic mild equation u(t)=S(t)x+∫_0^t S(t-r)f(u(r))dr+Φ(t), with Φ∈C_0([0,T],E), has a unique solution u in C((0,T],H)∩C((0,T],E), and in C([0,T],E) if x∈E. Assumption A4 is a uniform nonexplosion and continuity condition for this deterministic solution map: on bounded E-balls and bounded driving paths it remains uniformly bounded on bounded time intervals, and u(.,x_n,Φ_n)→u(.,x,Φ) in C_0([0,T],E) whenever x_n→x in E and Φ_n→Φ. Assumption A5 is H-strong Feller: for every t>0 and Borel A⊂H, P(t,x_n,A)→P(t,x,A) whenever x_n→x in H. Assumption A6 is full support of the stochastic convolution: for every T>0, the Gaussian law of Z on C_0([0,T],E) has support equal to all of C_0([0,T],E).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the paper's abstract framework rather than a theorem. The assumptions separate the roles needed later: A1-A3 give existence of mild solutions as X(t)=u(t,x,Z); A4 gives deterministic continuity and small-time control; A5 is used to pass continuity to hitting probabilities and embedded chains; A6 is used to steer the system along deterministic paths and obtain irreducibility.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A3 in the standing framework. Then there exists a unique mild solution X to dX=(AX+f(X))dt+dW, meaning an H-continuous adapted process satisfying X(t)=S(t)x+∫_0^t S(t-r)f(X(r))dr+Z(t). Moreover, the family of solutions with initial states x∈E defines a homogeneous Markov process on E, and therefore also on H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The stochastic convolution Z is E-valued and E-continuous by A2. Given a realization of Z, A3 gives a unique deterministic mild solution u(.,x,Z) to the integral equation, so define X(t)=u(t,x,Z). The nonanticipativity and independent-increment property in A2 imply the Markov property. The result is cited from Zabczyk's theorem on such pathwise mild formulations.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f:E→H be globally Lipschitz. Suppose S(t) maps H boundedly into E for every t>0 and, for each T>0, there are c>0 and α∈(0,1) such that ||S(t)||_{L(H,E)} ≤ c t^{-α} for t∈(0,T). Then the deterministic well-posedness condition A3b and the uniform boundedness and continuous-dependence condition A4 hold.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For fixed x∈H and driving path Φ∈C_0([0,T],E), solve the deterministic mild equation by a contraction argument in the weighted Banach space of E-valued functions on (0,T] with norm sup_{0<t≤T} t^α ||Y(t)||. The map A(Y)(t)=S(t)x+∫_0^t S(t-r)f(Y(r))dr+Φ(t) maps this space into itself because ||S(t)||_{H→E}≤ct^{-α} and ∫_0^t (t-r)^{-α}r^{-α}dr has the needed behavior for α<1 after the paper's chosen weighting. Introducing an equivalent exponentially weighted norm sup e^{-bt}t^α||Y(t)|| and taking b large makes A a contraction. Uniform bounds on bounded sets and continuous dependence on x and Φ follow from the generalized Gronwall lemma applied to the inequality ||u(t,x_n,Φ_n)-u(t,x,Φ)|| ≤ K(||x_n-x||+||Φ_n-Φ|| + ∫_0^t (t-r)^{-α}||u(r,x_n,Φ_n)-u(r,x,Φ)||dr). Continuity at t=0 in H, and in E when x∈E, follows because the convolution term tends to zero using the same singular-kernel estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A4. Let {e_i} be an orthonormal basis of H contained in D(A), let π_n be projection onto span{e_1,...,e_n}, set A_n=π_nAπ_n and S_n(t)=exp(tA_n). Define Q_t=∫_0^t S(r)QS*(r)dr, Q_{t,n}=∫_0^t S_n(r)π_nQπ_nS_n*(r)dr, B_t=Q_t^{-1/2}S(t), and B_{t,n}=Q_{t,n}^{-1/2}S_n(t)π_n. If the basis can be chosen so that for every T>0, sup_{0≤t≤T}|S_n(t)π_nx-S(t)x|→0 for all x∈H, sup_n sup_{0≤t≤T}|S_n(t)|_{L(H)}<∞, and sup_n |B_{t,n}|_{L(H)}+|B_t|_{L(H)}≤C t^{-β} for t∈(0,T] with some β∈(0,1), then the transition probabilities of X satisfy A5, the strong Feller property in H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: When f is extendable to a bounded globally Lipschitz map on H, the result follows from Da Prato and Zabczyk's smoothing estimates for the backward Kolmogorov equation associated with the Galerkin approximations. These estimates yield a total variation Lipschitz bound Var(P(t,x,.)-P(t,y,.))≤C_t|x-y|, with the singularity controlled by t^{-β}. For a merely locally Lipschitz f on bounded E-sets satisfying A3-A4, the paper indicates using truncation: replace f by bounded globally Lipschitz approximations agreeing with f on large E-balls, apply the estimate, and pass to the original equation using the nonexplosion and continuity controls.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A2. Define the deterministic control operator J:L^2(0,T;H)→C_0([0,T],H) by Jγ(t)=∫_0^t S(t-r)Q^{1/2}γ(r)dr. If either D1 holds, namely Q^{1/2}∈L(H,E) and the range of Q^{1/2} is dense in E, or D2 holds, namely S(t)∈L(H,E) for t>0 and ||S(t)||_{L(H,E)}≤h(t) on (0,T) for some h∈L^2(0,T), then A6 holds: the law of the stochastic convolution Z on C_0([0,T],E) has full support for every T>0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The reproducing kernel Hilbert space of the Gaussian law of Z on C_0([0,T],E) is the range of J, equipped with the infimum L^2-control norm. Therefore full support is equivalent to density of the range of J in C_0([0,T],E). The paper notes that each of D1 and D2 implies the needed density and continuity properties of J: D1 because instantaneous Q^{1/2}-directions are dense in E, and D2 because smoothing by S(t) places controlled paths in E with L^2 time integrability.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A4 and A6. Let P(t,x,.) be the transition probability of the mild solution X. Then P is irreducible in E: for every x∈H, every t>0, and every nonempty open set U⊂E, one has P(t,x,U)>0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First take x∈E and a ball U={v∈E:||v-z||<r}. Construct a deterministic E-continuous path Φ on [0,t] joining x to z, and define the control-like path ξ(s)=Φ(s)-S(s)x-∫_0^s S(s-u)f(Φ(u))du. By A4b, if an E-valued driving path e is close enough to ξ in C_0([0,t],E), then the deterministic solution u(t,x,e) lies within r of z. A6 gives P(||Z-ξ||<ε)>0, hence P(t,x,U)>0. For general x∈H, use that by A3 the solution is in E at every positive time, so for any 0<τ<t, P(t,x,U)=∫_E P(t-τ,y,U)P(τ,x,dy)>0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider dX(t)=(AX(t)+g(X(t)))dt+dW_t in H, where g:H→H is globally Lipschitz, and assume A1. Let v∈C^{1,2}((0,T)×H) be such that (v_x(t,x),Ax), initially for x∈D(A), extends to a continuous function h on [0,T]×H; v_{xx}(t,x) is nuclear; and for some k,p>0, |v_t(t,x)|+|v(t,x)|+|v_x(t,x)|+|v_{xx}(t,x)|_1+h(t,x)≤k(1+|x|^p). Then for 0<s<t<T, v(t,X(t))-v(s,X(s)) equals ∫_s^t [v_t(u,X(u))+h(u,X(u))+(g(X(u)),v_x(u,X(u)))+0.5 Tr(v_{xx}(u,X(u))Q)]du + ∫_s^t (v_x(u,X(u)),dW_u).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is an infinite-dimensional Itô formula adapted to possibly non-nuclear driving covariance Q. The usual trace term is still meaningful because v_{xx}(t,x) is nuclear. The polynomial growth bounds give integrability. The paper cites Ichikawa's Itô formula, noting that the non-nuclearity of Q is compensated by nuclearity of v_{xx}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting of Lemma 2.1, suppose v∈C^{1,2}(R_+×H) satisfies the same hypotheses on every finite interval. Let U be an open domain in H and x∈U. Assume v>0 on R_+×U and that Lv(s,y)≤-a(s) for (s,y)∈(0,T)×(D(A)∩U), where a>0 and b(t)=∫_0^t a(s)ds tends to ∞ as t→∞. Here Lv(s,y)=(Ay,v_x(s,y))+(g(y),v_x(s,y))+v_t(s,y)+0.5 Tr(v_{xx}(s,y)Q). If τ is the exit time of X from U, then P_x(τ<∞)=1 and E_x b(τ)≤v(0,x).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Stop X at τ, at the exit time τ_n from the H-ball of radius n, and at deterministic time t. Apply Lemma 2.1 to v at the bounded stopping time min(τ,τ_n,t). The local martingale has zero expectation and the inequality Lv≤-a gives E_x b(min(τ,τ_n,t))≤v(0,x). Let n→∞ and then t→∞, using monotone convergence and Fatou, to get E_x b(τ)≤v(0,x). Since b(t)→∞, τ must be finite almost surely.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A3. If U is a bounded open subset of E and τ_U is the exit time of X from U, then sup_{x∈U} E_x τ_U < ∞.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a globally Lipschitz map g:H→H agreeing with f on U, and choose a bounded open H-domain W containing U. The exit time from U for the original equation is bounded by the exit time from W for the globally Lipschitz equation dX=(AX+g(X))dt+dW. Apply Lemma 2.2 with a one-dimensional polynomial-exponential Lyapunov function built from a vector z∈D(A*) with z≠0. Pick d so that (x,z)+d>0 on W, choose a high polynomial power n so that the second-order noise contribution in the z-direction dominates the drift term on W, choose a positive constant c making the Lyapunov function positive on W, and then choose the exponential parameter small enough to obtain Lv≤-k e^{γt}. Lemma 2.2 then yields a uniform exponential-moment bound for the exit time from W, hence a uniform first-moment bound for τ_U.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A4. For every bounded set A⊂E there exists a centered open ball W in E containing A such that lim_{t↓0} sup_{x∈A} P_x(τ_W<t)=0, where τ_W is the exit time of X from W.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose R with A contained in the E-ball U_R. By A4a there is M>R such that the deterministic solution u(t,x,Φ) remains in the E-ball U_M for t∈[0,R] whenever ||x||≤R and ||Φ||_{C([0,R],E)}≤R. The stochastic convolution Z is E-continuous and Z(0)=0, so P(sup_{0≤s≤t}||Z(s)||>R)→0 as t↓0. Since X(t)=u(t,x,Z), taking W=U_M gives the claimed uniform small-time exit estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.5 and recurrence convention
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For A⊂E, τ^A denotes the first hitting time of the E-closure of A by X. If U⊂E is open and nonempty, X is called U-recurrent if P_x(τ^U<∞)=1 for every x∈E. It is called recurrent if it is U-recurrent for every nonempty open U⊂E. Under A1-A3, if X is U-recurrent in this sense, then the same hitting probability identity holds for every initial x∈H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For x∈H and any t>0, A3 gives P(t,x,E)=1. Applying the Markov property at time t gives P_x(τ^U<∞)≥∫_E P_y(τ^U<∞)P(t,x,dy)=1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Embedded-cycle Markov chain construction before Lemma 2.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A5 and suppose X is U-recurrent for a bounded open set U⊂E. Choose an open E-ball W containing U with the small-time property of Lemma 2.4. Let B be the E-closure of U. Define τ_0 as the first hitting time of B; define σ_1=inf{t>τ_0:X(t)∉W}; define τ_1=inf{t>σ_1:X(t)∈B}; and inductively σ_{n+1}=inf{t>τ_n:X(t)∉W}, τ_{n+1}=inf{t>σ_{n+1}:X(t)∈B}. Then τ_{n-1}<σ_n<τ_n almost surely and Y_n=X(τ_n), n∈N, is a homogeneous Markov chain. Its state space is the E-boundary ∂U=Cl_E(U)∖U after excursions from outside W back to Cl_E(U).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Continuity of paths gives σ_n>τ_{n-1}. Lemma 2.3 gives finiteness of exits from W, and U-recurrence gives finiteness of returns to Cl_E(U). The strong Markov property at the stopping times τ_n and σ_n, together with Maruyama's theorem on stopped strong Markov processes, yields a homogeneous Markov chain.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A5 and suppose X is U-recurrent for a bounded open set U⊂E. Let Y_n=X(τ_n) be the embedded Markov chain from the cycle construction, and let p(x,A)=P_x(Y_1∈A) be its transition kernel. Then the chain is strongly Feller in H: for every n∈N and every Borel A⊂H, p^n(.,A) is continuous on H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a bounded measurable φ on H, set Φ(x)=E_x φ(X(τ_n)). Decompose Φ according to whether τ_n≤t or τ_n>t. The part with τ_n≤t is uniformly small as t↓0 by Lemma 2.4 and the construction of the cycles. For the part with τ_n>t, fix 0<u<t and write it as E_x κ_{t,u}(X(u))=∫κ_{t,u}(z)P(u,x,dz), where κ_{t,u}(z)=E_z[φ(X(τ_n))1_{τ_n>t-u}] is bounded and measurable. A5 makes x↦∫κ_{t,u}dP(u,x,.) continuous. Letting t↓0 shows Φ is a uniform limit of continuous functions, hence continuous.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A5 and suppose X is U-recurrent for a bounded open set U⊂E. Then the embedded Markov chain Y_n=X(τ_n) has a stationary distribution μ_hat supported on ∂U.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 2.7 the chain is Feller, indeed strong Feller. Its values lie in ∂U, and the H-closure of ∂U is compact because U is bounded in E and E embeds compactly into H. A Feller Markov chain on a compact state space has an invariant probability measure. Since the chain actually takes values in ∂U, this invariant measure is supported on ∂U.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A6 and suppose X is U-recurrent for a bounded open set U⊂E. Then X has a σ-finite invariant measure μ on B(H). More explicitly, if μ_hat is an invariant probability measure for the embedded chain on ∂U, and τ(A)=∫_0^{τ_1} 1_A(X(t))dt is the time spent in A during one cycle starting from ∂U, then μ(A)=∫_{∂U} E_x τ(A) dμ_hat(x), A∈B(H), defines a σ-finite invariant measure for X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The definition gives a measure by monotone convergence. To prove σ-finiteness, it is enough to show μ(A)<∞ for sets A=Cl_H(G) with G bounded in E. Proposition 1.5 gives positive hitting probabilities of U from boundary points, and A5 plus compactness of relevant H-closures upgrades these to positive uniform lower bounds on compact boundary pieces. Strong Markov arguments then yield a time t>0 and s<1 such that sup_{x∈A}P_x(τ(A)>t)≤s, where τ(A) denotes occupation time of A in a cycle. Define the stopping time κ(t)=inf{v>τ_0:∫_{τ_0}^v 1_A(X(u))du=t}; on {τ(A)>t}, X(κ(t))∈A. Iterating the strong Markov property at κ(t) gives sup_{x∈A}P_x(τ(A)>nt)≤s^n, hence sup_{x∈A}E_xτ(A)<∞. This proves finite mass on bounded E-pieces, hence σ-finiteness. Invariance is the standard regenerative-cycle calculation of Khasminskii: the expected occupation measure over one cycle is invariant because the distribution of the chain at successive return times is μ_hat and μ_hat is stationary.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A6. Suppose there exists a bounded open set U⊂E such that, for every bounded set G⊂E, sup_{x∈G}E_x τ^U<∞, where τ^U is the first hitting time of Cl_E(U). Then X has an invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 2.9 constructs a σ-finite invariant measure as an expected occupation measure over cycles. The additional positive-recurrence condition gives finite expected return time to U uniformly on bounded E-sets. Together with Lemma 2.3, this implies that the total mass μ(H) of the occupation measure is finite. Normalizing μ by μ(H) gives an invariant probability measure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A6 and suppose X is U_R-recurrent for the E-ball U_R={x∈E:||x||<R}. Let Y_n be the embedded Markov chain associated with U_R. Then for every n∈N, every x∈∂U_R, and every nonempty set V⊂∂U_R open in the topology induced from E, one has p^n(x,V)>0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Chapman-Kolmogorov it suffices to prove n=1. Choose y∈V and a time T>0. Let r be so large that the auxiliary ball W used in the cycle construction is contained in U_r. Build a continuous E-path φ on [0,T] such that φ(0)=x, it moves out beyond W, then approaches y, and its final boundary hitting lies in V; explicitly the paper uses points x_1=(2r/R)x and y_1=(2r/R)y and a piecewise linear curve with ||φ(t)||>r on the middle third of the interval. Define ξ(t)=φ(t)-S(t)x-∫_0^t S(t-u)f(φ(u))du. By A4b, any driving path γ close to ξ forces u(.,x,γ) to follow φ closely, hence to realize the desired excursion and return to V. A6 gives positive probability that Z is that close to ξ, so p(x,V)>0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A6 and suppose X is U-recurrent for a bounded open set U⊂E. Let μ be the σ-finite invariant measure constructed in Theorem 2.9. If φ,ψ:H→R are μ-integrable Borel functions and ∫ψ dμ≠0, then for every x∈H, P_x[lim_{T→∞} (∫_0^T φ(X(t))dt)/(∫_0^T ψ(X(t))dt) = (∫φ dμ)/(∫ψ dμ)] = 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Step 1 proves ergodicity of the embedded chain Y. Lemma 2.7 gives strong Feller and Lemma 3.1 gives irreducibility, so Doob's theorem yields the strong law (1/n)∑_{i=1}^n ξ(Y_i)→∫_{∂U}ξ dμ_hat for μ_hat-a.e. initial point and every μ_hat-integrable ξ; equivalence of p^n(x,.) and μ_hat extends it to all starting boundary points, and also gives p^n(x,A)→μ_hat(A). Step 2 starts X with initial distribution μ_hat on ∂U and considers cycle occupation variables β_i=∫_{τ_i}^{τ_{i+1}}φ(X(t))dt. These form a stationary ergodic sequence, so n^{-1}∑β_i→∫φ dμ. If k(T) is the number of completed cycles before T, then k(T)^{-1}∫_0^T φ(X(t))dt→∫φdμ, and the same for ψ; taking the ratio gives the assertion for μ_hat-a.e. boundary starting point. Step 3 transfers the result to arbitrary x∈H by conditioning on Y_m and using the equivalence of p^m(x,.) with μ_hat.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A6. If X is U-recurrent for one bounded open set U⊂E, then X is recurrent, meaning it is G-recurrent for every nonempty open set G⊂E.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let μ be the invariant measure from Theorem 2.9. For any nonempty bounded open G⊂E, irreducibility from Proposition 1.5 and invariance imply μ(G)>0. Applying the cycle ergodic theorem to 1_G shows that, for μ_hat-a.e. boundary starting point, the total occupation time of G is infinite, so G is hit almost surely. Equivalence of embedded-chain transition probabilities transfers this hitting property to all x∈H. General nonempty open sets contain bounded nonempty open subsets, so the conclusion follows.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A6 and suppose X is recurrent. Let μ be the σ-finite invariant measure from Theorem 2.9. If μ(H)<∞, then μ*=μ/μ(H) is an invariant probability measure and for every x∈H and every μ*-integrable φ, P_x[lim_{T→∞} T^{-1}∫_0^T φ(X(t))dt = ∫φ dμ*]=1; moreover P(T,x,A)→μ*(A) for every x∈H and every Borel A⊂H. If μ(H)=∞, then for every μ-integrable φ, every x∈H, and every ε>0, P_x[|T^{-1}∫_0^T φ(X(t))dt|>ε]→0; in particular, for every bounded measurable D⊂E, T^{-1}∫_0^T P(t,x,D)dt→0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In the finite-mass case, apply Theorem 3.2 with ψ=1 to get the strong law of large numbers. Strong Feller plus irreducibility imply equivalence of P(t,x,.) and μ* for t>0, and Doob's convergence theorem yields P(T,x,A)→μ*(A). In the infinite-mass case, argue by contradiction: if time averages of a nonnegative integrable φ stayed above a fixed level with positive probability along T_n→∞, choose measurable sets M_k with μ(M_k)<∞ and μ(M_k)→∞. The ratio theorem applied to φ and 1_{M_k} contradicts the assumed lower bound for k large. The convergence of averaged transition probabilities for bounded D follows by applying the preceding assertion to φ=1_D and using the σ-finiteness estimate from Theorem 2.9 that μ(D)<∞.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A6 and suppose X is not recurrent. Then for every x∈H and every bounded measurable D⊂E, ∫_0^∞ P(t,x,D)dt<∞ and P(t,x,D)→0 as t→∞.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a bounded open U⊂E containing D that witnesses nonrecurrence. There exists z with positive probability never to hit U. By A5 this nonhitting probability is continuous on E in the needed sense, so there is a nonempty open V⊂E and r>0 such that starting from V the probability of never hitting U is at least r. By relative compactness of U in H, Proposition 1.5 and A5 give a uniform positive lower bound v for hitting V from Cl_E(U) in a fixed time. Strong Markov arguments then show that successive returns to U are dominated by a geometric tail with parameter rv, implying finite expected occupation time of U and hence of D. To prove P(t,x,D)→0, suppose instead P(t_n,x,U_L)>δ for some E-ball U_L and t_n→∞. A4a gives a larger ball U_M such that solutions starting in U_L stay in U_M for times s∈[0,1] when the noise path is small, and A6 gives a positive probability of such a small noise path. Thus P(t_n+s,x,U_M) is bounded below uniformly for s∈[0,1], contradicting the already proved integrability of t↦P(t,x,U_M).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A6 and suppose X is recurrent. Let μ be the σ-finite invariant measure from Theorem 2.9. Then for every x∈H and every Borel U⊂H with μ(U)>0, ∫_0^∞ P(t,x,U)dt=∞. In fact the proof gives P_x[∫_0^∞1_U(X(t))dt=∞]=1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For 0<μ(U)<∞, start X with the embedded-chain invariant distribution μ_hat. The cycle strong law gives n^{-1}∑_{i=0}^{n-1}∫_{τ_i}^{τ_{i+1}}1_U(X(t))dt→μ(U)>0, so the total occupation time of U is infinite almost surely. This implies the same assertion for μ_hat-a.e. boundary starting point. Equivalence of μ_hat with p^2(x,.) for arbitrary x transfers the almost sure infinite occupation statement to all x∈H. Sets of infinite μ-measure are handled by applying the argument to a positive finite-measure subset.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A6. If X is recurrent, then there exists a unique σ-finite invariant measure for X, up to multiplication by a positive constant. If X is not recurrent, then there is no finite invariant measure. Consequently, within this abstract framework, the Markov process has at most one invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the recurrent case, Proposition 3.6 gives the Harris-type infinite-potential condition for all sets of positive μ-measure. Strong Feller and irreducibility imply equivalence of transition probabilities, hence equivalence of resolvents, so the abstract uniqueness theorem in the Appendix applies and gives uniqueness up to scalar. For the nonrecurrent case, Proposition 3.5 gives T^{-1}∫_0^T P(t,x,D)dt→0 for each bounded D⊂E. If ν were a finite invariant measure, dominated convergence would give ν(D)=lim_{T→∞}T^{-1}∫_0^T∫P(t,x,D)dν(x)dt=0 for all bounded D⊂E. Since E is the union of bounded balls, this forces ν=0, contradicting that ν is a finite invariant measure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition A.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (E,Sigma) be a measurable space and let P_t(x,.) be Markov transition kernels satisfying measurability in (t,x), Chapman-Kolmogorov, and the usual kernel properties. Define T_t f(x)=∫f(y)P_t(x,dy) on bounded measurable f and the resolvent U(x,A)=∫_0^∞ e^{-t}P_t(x,A)dt. Assume all measures U(x,.) are equivalent. Let μ be a σ-finite invariant measure. Suppose there exists a strictly positive bounded measurable f with ∫f dμ<∞ and ∫_0^∞ T_t f(x)dt=∞ for every x∈E. Then every σ-finite invariant measure is a constant multiple of μ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix x_0 and put m=U(x_0,.). By equivalence of resolvents, every σ-finite invariant measure is equivalent to m. Let ρ be another σ-finite invariant measure. Choose a strictly positive bounded h with finite integrals under μ and ρ, and choose c>0 so that ν=cρ satisfies ∫h dν=∫h dμ. Write μ=um and ν=vm with finite densities. Let η=(μ-ν)^+=(u-v)^+m. Minimality of the Jordan decomposition implies T_t^*η≥η. A telescoping estimate using invariance of μ and ν shows that for each fixed t>0, ∫_0^T <T_t^*η-η,T_s f>ds stays bounded uniformly in T. Since T_t^*η-η is nonnegative and ∫_0^∞T_s f(x)ds=∞ at every point, Tonelli forces T_t^*η=η. Thus η is invariant. If η=0, then μ≤ν and the equality of h-integrals gives μ=ν. If η≠0, invariance and resolvent equivalence make η equivalent to m; hence the set {u>v} has full m-measure, contradicting ∫h dμ=∫h dν. Therefore μ=ν, up to the scalar c.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H have orthonormal basis {e_i}, let Ae_i=-α_i e_i with α_i→∞ and α_i>0, and let Qe_i=λ_i e_i with 0<λ_i≤λ_0. Then A1 is equivalent to ∑_i λ_i/α_i<∞. For E_a=D((-A)^a) with graph norm, if ∑_i λ_i α_i^{2ε-1}<∞ for some ε>0, then A2 holds for 0<a<ε. The support assumption A6 holds if either a<1/2 or the sequence α_i^a λ_i^{1/2} is bounded. A strong Feller sufficient condition is that sup_i sqrt(α_i)/sqrt(λ_i(e^{2α_i t}-1)) ≤ c t^{-β} on small t for some β∈(0,1). If f:E_a→H satisfies the local bounded-set Lipschitz condition A3a and the global E_a-Lipschitz bound |f(x)-f(y)|≤C||x-y||_{E_a}, with 0<a<min(1/2,ε), then all assumptions A1-A6 hold and the paper's invariant-measure and ergodic conclusions apply.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The diagonal form makes the Hilbert-Schmidt stochastic convolution condition and smoothing estimates explicit. A1 follows by summing ∫_0^T e^{-2α_i t}λ_i dt. Regularity in E_a is obtained by inserting α_i^{2a} into the same series, yielding the stated stronger summability. The support condition follows from Proposition 1.4, and strong Feller from Proposition 1.3 after computing Q_t^{-1/2}S(t) on basis elements. Proposition 1.2 supplies A3b and A4 for globally E_a-Lipschitz f.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 3.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider the system ∂_t u_i(t,x)=Δu_i(t,x)+F_i(u_1(t,x),...,u_n(t,x))+η_i(t,x), i=1,...,n, on (0,π) with Dirichlet boundary conditions and globally Lipschitz F:R^n→R^n, driven by independent space-time white noises. In the abstract formulation H=(L^2(0,π))^n, A is the Dirichlet Laplacian componentwise, Q=I, and f is the Nemytskii map induced by F. The paper verifies A1-A6 for E=E_a=D((-A)^a) with 0<a<1/4. It also verifies A1-A6 for E=V_δ=[h_0^δ[0,π]]^n, 0<δ<1/4, where h_0^δ is the separable little Hölder space with zero boundary condition. Therefore all main conclusions, including at most one stationary distribution and the recurrence dichotomy, apply to this stochastic reaction-diffusion system.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the sine basis e_{i,j}; the Dirichlet Laplacian eigenvalues grow like i^2 and Q=I. The series ∑_i i^{4ε}∫_0^T e^{-2i^2t}dt is finite for ε<1/4, giving the stochastic convolution regularity needed for E_a. The strong Feller bound follows from the explicit estimate 1/sqrt(e^{2i^2t}-1)≤C t^{-1/2} after optimizing over i. The Nemytskii drift is globally Lipschitz on H because F is globally Lipschitz. For the Hölder choice E=V_δ, the paper uses separability of little Hölder spaces, strong continuity of the heat semigroup on V_δ, smoothing estimates S(t):L^2→C_0^μ with t-singularity integrable for μ<1/4, and Proposition 1.4 to get full support.

proof_techniques.name: Compactly embedded path space to recover compactness
proof_techniques.description: The paper does not work solely in the Hilbert state space H. It assumes solutions immediately live in a separable Banach space E compactly embedded into H and have continuous E-valued paths. This compact embedding is used when bounded sets in E have compact closure in H, which allows the embedded return chain to be treated as a Feller chain on a compact H-state space and guarantees existence of an invariant distribution for that chain.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a Dirichlet stochastic heat equation, one can try to use smoothing to place the process in a compactly embedded subspace such as a little Hölder space or a fractional Sobolev domain. This can replace local compactness of the full infinite-dimensional state space.

proof_techniques.name: Support-control proof of irreducibility
proof_techniques.description: Irreducibility is obtained by deterministic steering. Given initial point x, target open ball around z, and time t, construct a continuous path Φ from x to z and define a forcing path ξ that would make Φ solve the deterministic mild equation. Continuity of the deterministic solution map says that noise paths close to ξ reach the target, and full support of the Gaussian stochastic convolution makes this event have positive probability.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For additive space-time white noise with Dirichlet heat kernel, the Gaussian convolution often has full support in suitable path spaces. If the solution map for the singular drift can be approximated or shown continuous in a weak enough topology, this gives a concrete route to topological irreducibility.

proof_techniques.name: Khasminskii embedded-cycle construction
proof_techniques.description: Starting from a bounded recurrent open set U, the paper chooses a larger ball W and defines alternating stopping times: returns to Cl_E(U) and exits from W. The boundary return points form a Markov chain Y_n. An invariant measure for the continuous-time process is then built as the expected occupation measure during one cycle, averaged over a stationary distribution of Y_n.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is useful when an invariant measure is hard to identify explicitly. If one can establish recurrence of some bounded set for the SPDE, the cycle construction can produce and analyze invariant measures without solving a stationary equation.

proof_techniques.name: Strong Feller property transferred to hitting chains
proof_techniques.description: The embedded chain's transition kernel involves random hitting times, not deterministic times. The paper proves it is strong Feller by decomposing expectations according to whether the hitting time occurs before a small deterministic time. The early-hit term is uniformly small by a small-time exit estimate; the remaining term is a deterministic-time semigroup applied to a bounded measurable function and is continuous by the strong Feller property of the original process.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If strong Feller estimates are available for the SPDE or its approximations, this technique can extend them to return-time chains used in recurrence arguments. It avoids differentiating or estimating the hitting distribution directly.

proof_techniques.name: One-cycle occupation measure as invariant measure
proof_techniques.description: Given a stationary law μ_hat for the embedded boundary chain, define μ(A)=∫E_x∫_0^{τ_1}1_A(X(t))dt dμ_hat(x). The regenerative structure implies invariance: after one complete cycle, the boundary distribution is again μ_hat, so time-shifted occupations have the same averaged law. σ-finiteness is proved by showing that occupation times of bounded E-sets have geometric tails, using irreducibility, strong Feller continuity, and compactness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For uniqueness questions, such a constructed measure can serve as the canonical σ-finite invariant measure to compare all others against. The geometric occupation estimate may be adaptable if bounded subsets of the solution space are compact in a weaker topology.

proof_techniques.name: Ratio ergodic theorem via cycle occupations
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves a Hopf-type ratio theorem by applying the strong law of large numbers to the embedded Markov chain and to cycle occupation variables β_i=∫_{τ_i}^{τ_{i+1}}φ(X(t))dt. For two μ-integrable observables φ and ψ, the ratio of their long-time integrals converges almost surely to the ratio of their μ-integrals.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Once a σ-finite invariant measure and recurrent cycle structure are available, this gives ergodic consequences without requiring the invariant measure to be finite. It can be used to prove Harris recurrence and uniqueness of invariant probabilities.

proof_techniques.name: Recurrent/nonrecurrent dichotomy for invariant probabilities
proof_techniques.description: Under A1-A6, the paper proves that recurrence implies uniqueness of the σ-finite invariant measure up to scalar, while nonrecurrence implies all bounded E-sets have finite potential and transition probabilities to them vanish; as a consequence, no finite invariant measure can exist in the nonrecurrent case. Thus the existence of any invariant probability forces the recurrent side of the dichotomy, where uniqueness holds.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the most direct strategic use for the original problem: to prove at most one invariant probability, it may suffice to verify the paper's structural assumptions rather than prove recurrence separately, because nonrecurrence would rule out invariant probabilities altogether.

proof_techniques.name: Appendix Harris uniqueness through equivalent resolvents
proof_techniques.description: The Appendix gives an abstract uniqueness criterion: if all resolvent measures U(x,.) are equivalent and one invariant σ-finite measure μ satisfies a Harris-type infinite-potential condition ∫_0^∞T_tf(x)dt=∞ for a strictly positive μ-integrable f, then μ is the only σ-finite invariant measure up to scalar. The proof compares two invariant measures by their densities with respect to a common resolvent measure and shows the positive part of their difference would itself be invariant, forcing a contradiction after normalization.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This criterion can be used independently of the full cycle construction. For the SPDE, proving equivalence of transition or resolvent laws plus a Harris recurrence condition for one invariant measure would directly imply uniqueness.

proof_techniques.name: Lyapunov exit-time estimate from a bounded domain
proof_techniques.description: To control cycles, the paper proves that expected exit times from bounded E-open sets are uniformly finite. The key is to replace the drift by a globally Lipschitz extension on a larger H-domain and apply an infinite-dimensional Itô formula to a Lyapunov function depending on one nondegenerate linear functional (x,z), with a high polynomial power chosen so the noise term forces exit.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For SPDEs with additive nondegenerate noise, analogous one-dimensional projection Lyapunov functions may provide uniform control of excursions from bounded sets, even when the full drift is difficult.

proof_techniques.name: Explicit verification for one-dimensional reaction-diffusion noise
proof_techniques.description: Example 3.10 checks the abstract assumptions for a Dirichlet heat operator on an interval with space-time white noise and globally Lipschitz Nemytskii drift. It uses sine eigenfunctions, heat semigroup smoothing into fractional domains or little Hölder spaces, full support of the stochastic convolution, and strong Feller estimates from the explicit covariance.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original SPDE has the same linear Dirichlet heat operator and additive space-time white noise. The example identifies natural compactly embedded spaces and the regularity thresholds that are likely relevant, though the singular drift requires additional justification beyond the paper.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly emphasizes that infinite-dimensional systems can have multiple stationary distributions or fail strong laws without additional compactness and nondegeneracy assumptions, unlike nonsingular finite-dimensional diffusions. Remark 3.9 shows in a diagonal linear example that if the strong Feller range condition fails, a unique stationary distribution may still exist while the strong law of large numbers fails; this underlines the role of equivalence/strong Feller hypotheses in the uniqueness and ergodicity theory. The cited sources most directly connected to the paper's tools are Da Prato and Zabczyk on smoothing and strong Feller properties, Khasminskii and Harris on recurrence and invariant measures, and Funaki/Manthey-Maslowski for stochastic reaction-diffusion invariant-measure results. For applying the paper to a singular-drift SPDE, the critical hypotheses to establish in the paper's language would be an H-strong Feller property, full support of the stochastic convolution or solution law in a compactly embedded E, and enough path regularity/continuity to run the cycle and irreducibility arguments.
why_relevant: This paper develops recurrence-based ergodic results for stochastic evolution equations. Such methods can help prove uniqueness of invariant measures when direct strong Feller estimates are hard to pass to a singular drift limit.
